title: Control of the Néel vector in the quantum antiferromagnetic honeycomb lattice
authors: Asliddin Khudoyberdiev, Dag-Björn Hering, Vanessa Sulaiman, Götz S. Uhrig
abstract: The switching of antiferromagnetic order and its efficient control promise to enable ultrafast manipulation of data and large storage capacity. Recently, the time-dependent Schwinger boson mean-field theory has been successfully developed to study the Néel vector switching in hypercubic antiferromagnetic lattices. In the present article, we aim at demonstrating that the approach is a well-justified framework to capture the essentials of the switching process, even in low-symmetry quantum antiferromagnets. To this end, we show the possibility of the sublattice magnetization reorientation in the quantum antiferromagnetic honeycomb lattice. First, equilibrium properties of the honeycomb lattice are analyzed using the Schwinger boson mean-field theory and compared to the continuous similarity transformation method to justify the applicability of the approach. Then, the Schwinger boson mean-field theory is employed for switching process. We provide a comprehensive answer to the question what the threshold switching fields are when the coordination number of the lattice is varied. Indeed, the results of the study reveal a correspondence between lattice structures and the threshold fields by comparing them for the square and the simple cubic lattices and the honeycomb lattice. The findings of the present article extend the foundation for future theoretical and computational advancements in the field of antiferromagnetic switching. These advancements are of particular relevance for the development of ultrafast spintronic or magnonic devices.
summary: This paper explores how to efficiently flip the magnetic orientation in a quantum antiferromagnetic honeycomb lattice. By using Schwinger boson mean-field theory, the authors show that applying a staggered magnetic field can trigger 'exchange-enhanced switching' at much lower energy costs than a uniform field. They also identify universal scaling laws that relate the required switching field to the lattice's coordination number. These findings are significant for the development of ultrafast, energy-efficient spintronic and magnonic devices.
main_problem: Investigating the control and switching of the Neel vector in a quantum antiferromagnetic honeycomb lattice, specifically determining the threshold magnetic fields required for magnetization reorientation.
main_result: The study establishes universal scaling laws for threshold fields in different lattices and demonstrates that staggered fields enable 'exchange-enhanced switching' at much lower magnitudes than uniform fields.
method: The authors use time-dependent Schwinger boson mean-field theory (SB-MFT) and Continuous Similarity Transformation (CST) to model the non-equilibrium switching process and validate equilibrium properties.
model_or_system: A spin-1/2 anisotropic XXZ Heisenberg model on a quantum antiferromagnetic honeycomb lattice, including both uniform and staggered external magnetic fields.
key_observables: Sublattice magnetization (Neel vector), spin gap, boson occupation numbers, and oscillation frequencies of the Neel vector.
important_parameters: Anisotropy parameter (chi), coordination number (z), exchange coupling (J), and external magnetic field strengths (hu and hs).
main_assumptions: The study focuses on the ground state at zero temperature and assumes the validity of the Schwinger boson mean-field approximation for low-symmetry lattices.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the switching process, the honeycomb lattice structure, spin gap and magnetization vs. anisotropy, temporal evolution of magnetization and boson occupation, and scaling laws for threshold fields across different lattice types.
paper_structure: The paper begins by validating the SB-MFT framework against CST and DMRG for equilibrium properties, then moves to modeling the non-equilibrium switching dynamics under uniform and staggered fields, and concludes with a comparative analysis of lattice coordination numbers and universal scaling laws.
why_interesting: The paper provides deep insights into many-body dynamics, specifically how collective modes (magnons) drive the reversal of an order parameter and how dephasing leads to steady-state convergence without explicit relaxation.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. This classical illustration shows how the sublattice mag- netization is manipulated. The initial state, with two linked red and blue sublattices, is shown on the left. The arrows inside the circles indicate the direction of the sublattice magnetization. A uniform magnetic field (gray arrows) is applied to switch the order (middle), and the final state is obtained (last two circles on the right). The round green arrows indicate the direction of the Larmor precession of the spins on the sublattice.

Fig. 2. Honeycomb lattice with two nonequivalent sites. The sub- lattice R correspond to site i and sublattice R′ correspond to site j. The vectors δl (l = 1, 2, 3) connect the site R to its three neigh- bouring R′ sites.

Fig. 3. The spin gap vs. the anisotropy parameter. The blue cir- cles with dotted line are obtained by the SB-MFT as employed in this article. The orange line displays results of the plain series expansions around the Ising limit from Oitmaa et al. in Ref. 31. The green line is the result of the second-order spin-wave analy- sis in Ref. 46. The red stars correspond to the CST 0n generator. The diamond shape markers are obtained by DM-MFT. The tri symbols belong to the DMRG method from Ref. 47.

Fig. 4. The sublattice magnetization dependence on the anisotropy parameter. The blue circles with dotted line depict the results of the SB-MFT. The orange line is generated from the results of the series expantions around the Ising limit from Oitmaa et al. in Ref. 31. The green line is the result of the second-order spin-wave analysis in Ref. 46. The red stars correspond to the CST 0n generator. The diamond shape markers are obtained by the DM-MFT. The tri symbols belong to the DMRG method from Ref. 47. It should be noted that the magnetization is always finite, even for χ = 1.

Fig. 5. The dynamics of the occupations of the bosons and the magnetization. The strength of the external magnetic field is hu = 0.645 J. The magenta colored dashed line shows the dynamics of the spin gap. The double-headed arrow shows the instant of the switching, i.e., the sign change of m(t). For comparison, the dashed green horizontal line is included to indicate the negative initial magnetization.

Fig. 7. The uniform threshold field and the spin gap dependence on the anisotropy parameter χ. The triangle markers indicate the values of hthr u while the tri markers show the value of the spin gap ∆. The solid lines are square root fits: hthr,α u,ﬁt = zαcα p

Fig. 6. The dynamics of the sublattice magnetization are shown for the range of anisotropies 0.8 ≤χ < 1. Panel a) shows the dynam- ics for the case where the external field is applied at all times t > 0. In panel b), the field is turned off after the switching at t1, where t1 is the time at which m(t) reaches its first minimum in the m(t) < 0 region. The minimum value of the external field for switching is labeled by the superscript “thr” for threshold. For all anisotropies, the field strength is chosen 5% higher than the threshold value to ensure reliable switching.

Fig. 8. The classical illustration of the exchange-enhanced switching. The initial state, with two linked red and blue sublat- tices, is shown on the left. The arrows inside the circles indicate the direction of the sublattice magnetization. Subsequent to the ap- plication of the staggered magnetic fields on the sublattices (gray arrows), the spins exhibit a slight deflection, concurrently forming a robust effective field (black arrow). Consequently, the spins ro- tate around the resulting effective field (shown by the green curved arrows) and the final switched state is obtained (last two circles on the right).

Fig. 9. The staggered threshold field dependence on the anisotropy parameter χ. The triangle markers correspond to the hthr s values while the solid lines are linear fits: hthr,α s,ﬁt = zαdα(1 −χ), α ∈{sc, sq, hc} where zα is the coordination number of the lattice and dα are the fit parameters with dsc = (0.228 ± 0.0007)J (simple cubic lattice zsc = 6), dsq = (0.208 ± 0.0016)J (square lattice zsq = 4) and dhc = (0.191±0.0019)J (honeycomb lattice zhc = 3). The data for the square and simple cubic lattices are taken from Ref. 15.

Fig. 10. The dynamics of the sublattice magnetization under staggered switching field in the anisotropy range χ ∈[0.8, 1). Panel a) shows the dynamics when the field is active at t > 0 whereas panel b) shows the dynamics for the case when the field is applied only till the magnetization reaches its first minimum. The strength of the field is chosen always 5% higher than the threshold value of the field at the given anisotropy.
